title: Turmeric on Carpet
timeEstimate: 30–90 minutes plus multi-day sunlight finisher; honest cap on dyed carpet
stainChemistry: Turmeric on carpet is the toughest yellow-dye case in common use because the bright yellow turmeric pigment grips fibers fast and resists conventional oxidation. The recurring traps: (1) chlorine bleach (turns the turmeric yellow into RED-BROWN — worse than original), (2) alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide all DARKEN turmeric permanently), and (3) hot water (sets the pigment). Carpet adds two more complications: pad saturation (over-wetting drives the yellow into the pad where you can't reach it), and dyed-carpet color-loss risk if you reach for peroxide without a hidden test. The saving feature on turmeric is photodegradation — UV light gradually breaks down the yellow color over hours-to-days. Honest cap: full removal on dyed carpet is often impossible. Manage expectations.
whyThisWorks: On wet curry: Dawn first to strip the oil carrier, then peroxide can reach the pigment. On dry turmeric (spice spill): the powder is the bulk — vacuum it dry first, then chemistry. Cool damp-cloth peroxide on hidden-test-passed synthetic carpet partially oxidizes the yellow. Sunlight is the real finisher — UV photodegrades turmeric over hours-to-days. Patience plus sunlight beats aggressive chemistry. The home protocol stops at oxygen-based oxidation (3% peroxide). Chlorine bleach is BANNED — produces a permanent red-brown reaction worse than the original yellow.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): turmeric's pigment is curcumin — a diketo-enol compound with a stable conjugated chromophore. Curcumin shifts color with pH (yellow in acid, RED-BROWN in alkali). Photodegradable under UV (250–400 nm). Pro uses NSD oil-strip + 3% H₂O₂ at controlled dilution + UV-assist + acetic acid neutralizing rinse. Sodium hydrosulfite is the pro nuclear option for set yellow on synthetic carpet.
defaultAssumption: Curry / turmeric spill on synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) home carpet. Wet curry = full sequence with Dawn oil-strip first. Dry turmeric (spice powder spill) = vacuum first. Wool carpet = pro from minute one. Honest cap: full removal on dyed carpet is often impossible.

spottingProtocol.agent: Triage — Wet Curry vs Dry Turmeric
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dry turmeric (powder spill): HEPA vacuum bulk dry — never wet first (water turns powder into a paste that spreads). Wet curry: scrape solids with plastic spoon edge into a garbage bag — never rub. Wool carpet = pro from minute one.

spottingProtocol.agent: Dawn + Soft Brush — Strip Oil (Wet Curry)
spottingProtocol.technique: Mandatory before peroxide on wet curry
spottingProtocol.instruction: Wet curry only: work Dawn into the spot with a soft toothbrush 5–10 min. Dawn emulsifies the oil carrier so peroxide can reach the pigment. Damp-cloth rinse — NEVER pour water (pad saturation). Skip on dry turmeric.
Hidden carpet area: dab 3% peroxide on a white cloth, press 2 min. Color transfers = skip peroxide. Sunlight + acid only finish.
Hidden-test-passed synthetic carpet: 3% peroxide damp cloth 5–10 min. Damp-cloth rinse. NEVER mix with chlorine bleach (turns yellow red-brown), ammonia, or alkaline cleaners (darken turmeric).

spottingProtocol.technique: Keep tannin on lighter side, neutralize peroxide
spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tbsp white vinegar + 1 cup cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Vinegar + cool water on damp cloth 5 min. Neutralizes peroxide AND keeps any tannin component on the lighter side of its color range. Damp-cloth rinse.

Open blinds so direct sunlight falls on the spot for several hours. UV breaks down turmeric pigment slowly. Multi-day sunlight is the most reliable finisher. For movable area rugs, take outdoors for direct sun.

spottingProtocol.agent: Weighted Paper Towels Overnight Plus Fan
spottingProtocol.instruction: Stack paper towels + weight overnight to extract residual moisture and pigment from the pad. Fan-dry. Inspect dry. Faint yellow remaining = repeat sunlight, not chemistry.

steps: 1. Triage — wet curry vs dry turmeric. Wool carpet = pro.
2. HEPA vacuum dry powder OR scrape wet curry solids gently.
3. Dawn oil-strip on wet curry — mandatory before peroxide.
5. 3% H₂O₂ damp cloth on hidden-test-passed synthetic carpet.
6. White vinegar acid rinse — neutralize peroxide.
7. Sunlight finisher — UV photodegrades turmeric over hours-to-days.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets turmeric pigment permanently
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach — turns turmeric yellow to RED-BROWN (worse than original)
NEVER alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide) — darken turmeric permanently
NEVER pour water — pad saturation drives yellow into pad
Wool carpet = pro from minute one
Hidden-area peroxide test mandatory on dyed carpet
Sunlight is the finisher — patience over hours-to-days
Honest cap: dyed carpet often plateaus at faint yellow shadow

homeSolutions.agent: Turmeric on Carpet Has an Honest Cap
homeSolutions.instruction: Turmeric is the toughest yellow-dye stain in common use. Three hard rules: NO chlorine bleach (turns the yellow RED-BROWN — worse than original), NO alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone all darken turmeric permanently), and NO hot water (sets the pigment). The path: oil-strip (wet curry only) → bounded peroxide on hidden-test-passed synthetic carpet → sunlight finisher over hours-to-days. Honest cap: full removal on dyed carpet is often impossible. Wool carpet = pro from minute one.

homeSolutions.agent: Dry Turmeric Powder vs Wet Curry — Different Starts
homeSolutions.instruction: Dry turmeric (spice spill): HEPA vacuum the bulk powder DRY first. Never wet it first — water turns the powder into a paste that spreads. Wet curry (sauce spill): scrape solid bulk with a plastic spoon edge into a garbage bag — never rub, rubbing pushes pigment into the carpet.

Wet curry only: work Dawn dish soap into the spot with a soft toothbrush for 5 to 10 minutes. The oil in curry holds the turmeric pigment in place — Dawn strips the oil so peroxide can reach the pigment underneath. Damp-cloth rinse — NEVER pour water on the carpet, that drives the yellow into the pad. Skip this step on dry turmeric powder.
In a hidden carpet area, dab 3% peroxide on a white cloth, press 2 minutes. Color transfers = skip peroxide entirely. No transfer = proceed.
Hidden-test-passed synthetic carpet only: 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp cloth, press the spot 5 to 10 minutes, blot. Partially oxidizes the yellow.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: NEVER mix peroxide with chlorine bleach (turns turmeric red-brown), ammonia (toxic chloramine gas), or alkaline cleaners (darken turmeric).

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 tablespoon white vinegar with 1 cup cool water. Apply with damp cloth, press 5 minutes. Neutralizes any peroxide AND keeps any tannin part of the stain on its lighter side. Damp-cloth rinse.

Open blinds so direct sunlight falls on the spot for several hours. UV breaks down turmeric pigment slowly — this is the most reliable finisher. Multi-day sunlight exposure is normal for stubborn yellow. For movable area rugs, take outside for direct sun. Patience over hours-to-days beats aggressive chemistry.

Stack paper towels on the spot, weight with a heavy book, leave overnight. Fan-dry in the morning. Pulls residual moisture and pigment out of the pile and prevents yellow wicking back up from the pad. Inspect dry — faint yellow remaining? Repeat sunlight, not more chemistry.

Full sequence + multi-day sunlight didn't fully clear it? On dyed carpet, that's often the honest cap — pro chemistry plateaus on this stain too. For wool carpet, pro from minute one. Tell them: "Turmeric on [synthetic / wool] carpet, age [fresh / hours / days], home-treated with [HEPA vacuum / Dawn oil-strip + 3% peroxide on hidden-test-passed + vinegar + sunlight × N days]. No chlorine, no alkaline, no hot, no over-wetting. [Residual yellow / honest cap reached]."

steps: 1. Set honest expectations — dyed carpet often plateaus at partial yellow shadow.
2. NO chlorine bleach. NO alkaline cleaners. NO hot water. NO over-wetting. Wool carpet = pro.
3. Triage — wet curry (scrape) vs dry turmeric (HEPA vacuum dry first).
4. Dawn oil-strip on wet curry only — MANDATORY before peroxide.
6. 3% peroxide damp cloth on hidden-test-passed synthetic carpet, 5–10 min.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on wool carpet (pro). Stop if hot water / steam / hot vacuum was used on the spot (often permanent). Stop if chlorine bleach was used (turned yellow to red-brown — irreversible). Stop if alkaline cleaner was used (darkened yellow permanently). Stop after full sequence + multi-day sunlight plateau on dyed carpet — accept honest cap.
warnings: NEVER chlorine bleach — RED-BROWN reaction worse than original yellow
NEVER alkaline cleaners — darken turmeric permanently
NEVER hot water or hot dryer — locks pigment
NEVER pour water — pad saturation drives pigment into pad
Wool carpet = pro from minute one
Hidden test on dyed carpet before peroxide
Sunlight is the finisher — patience over hours-to-days
Honest cap on dyed carpet — partial removal is the norm

neverDo: Never chlorine bleach on turmeric — turns yellow to red-brown permanently
Never alkaline cleaners on turmeric — darken permanently
Never hot water on turmeric + carpet
Never skip oil-strip on wet curry before peroxide
Never mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + OxiClean
fiberSensitivities: Synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) carpet: tolerates 3% peroxide on hidden-test-passed
Wool carpet: pro from minute one — peroxide yellows wool, alkaline lifts wool scales
Dyed carpet: hidden peroxide test mandatory; honest cap is partial removal
Carpet pad: cannot be cleaned in place — pad saturation = pad replacement
Turmeric pigment is the polyester-cotton-carpet-specific lock — UV photodegradation is the safest finisher

materialWarnings: Turmeric on carpet has an HONEST CAP — full removal often impossible on dyed
Chlorine bleach is the most common irreversible failure — yellow to red-brown
Pad saturation drives the yellow where you can't reach it
Sunlight is the finisher — patience over hours-to-days
Wool carpet = pro from minute one

escalation.when: Wool carpet from minute one. Hot water / steam / hot vacuum already used (often permanent). Chlorine bleach already used (turned yellow to red-brown — irreversible). Alkaline cleaner already used (darkened yellow permanently). Pad saturation visible (yellow bleed-through to pad). Full sequence + multi-day sunlight plateau on dyed carpet.
escalation.whatToTell: "Turmeric / curry on [synthetic / wool] carpet, [wet curry / dry powder / dried-in], age [fresh / hours / days], home-treated with [HEPA vacuum / Dawn oil-strip + 3% peroxide on hidden-test-passed + vinegar + sunlight × N days + weighted towels]. No chlorine, no alkaline, no hot, no over-wetting. [Residual yellow / red-brown reaction occurred / pad saturation suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with reducing-bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) chemistry for synthetic carpet; specialty wool carpet cleaner if wool

contextQuestions: Wet curry sauce or dry turmeric spice powder?
Synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) carpet or wool?
Has chlorine bleach been used (often produces red-brown lock)?
Has alkaline cleaner (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone) been used?
Has water been poured (pad saturation risk)?
Sunny location available for sunlight finisher?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Wet curry vs dry turmeric powder are different starting protocols — never wet dry powder, always Dawn oil-strip wet curry. Sunlight is genuinely the most reliable finisher because turmeric pigment is photodegradable. Honest cap on dyed carpet is real — pro chemistry plateaus too. Wool carpet adds banned-tools list (no broad-spectrum enzymes, no peroxide, no alkaline) and goes to pro from minute one. Pad saturation is the silent failure mode — always damp-cloth, never pour.
